title: Beyond Gaussian Statistics in Polymer Melts: Statistical Masking of Persistent Local Constraints
abstract: Short polymer chains exhibit clear deviations from Gaussian end-to-end distance statistics, yet the molecular mechanism by which Gaussian behavior is recovered in long chains remains unestablished. Atomistic molecular dynamics simulations of polyethylene melts reveal that conformational heterogeneity persists at the Kuhn scale across all chain lengths, consisting of a mosaic of slow-relaxing, extended aligned chain segments (ACS) and coiled segments -- random conformational sequences (RCS) and chain ends (CE). We show that the end-to-end distance distributions for both unentangled and entangled chains are accurately described by a $q$-Gaussian function, with the entropic index $q$ increasing systematically from $0.67$ (C50) to $0.99$ (C500). This evolution tracks the emergence and accumulation of RCS segments, which are absent in short chains, establishing $q$ as a quantitative ``heterogeneity index''. The $q < 1$ values are a signature of non-extensive statistics, with the ratio of Tsallis to Boltzmann-Gibbs entropy ($S_q/S_1$), computed directly from simulation data without fitting, decreasing from $1.80$ (C50) to $1.03$ (C500). Crucially, we demonstrate that Gaussian recovery does not result from the erasure of Kuhn-scale heterogeneities, as ACS domains persist in all chain lengths above the critical mass ($\approx 35\%$). Instead, the transition to Gaussian statistics is a statistical masking effect, where the accumulation of independent RCS segments progressively obscures the non-Gaussian signatures of the persistent ACS domains.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the transition from non-Gaussian to Gaussian statistics in polymer melts is not due to the disappearance of local structural heterogeneities, but rather because increasing chain length adds enough random segments to mask the underlying aligned segments. Using Tsallis statistics, the author quantifies this as a reduction in the entropic index q toward unity.
main_problem: Understanding the molecular mechanism behind the recovery of Gaussian statistics in long polymer chains and determining if this transition results from the erasure of local conformational heterogeneities.
main_result: The transition to Gaussian behavior is a statistical masking effect where the accumulation of random conformational segments (RCS) obscures persistent, non-Gaussian aligned chain segments (ACS), rather than the erasure of those segments.
method: Atomistic molecular dynamics simulations of polyethylene melts combined with a statistical analysis using the Tsallis non-extensive framework and q-Gaussian distributions.
model_or_system: Polyethylene melts with varying chain lengths (C50 to C500) modeled as a 3D mosaic of Aligned Chain Segments (ACS), Random Conformational Sequences (RCS), and Chain Ends (CE).
key_observables: End-to-end distance distribution P(R), entropic index q, entropy ratio S_q/S_1, tail concentration T2, and non-Gaussianity parameter alpha2.
important_parameters: Chain length (N), entropic index (q), Kuhn scale, and the mass fraction of ACS.
main_assumptions: The q-Gaussian model is applied within the range 0 < q < 5/3 to ensure finite moments in 3D; the system is studied in the NpT ensemble.
figures_summary: Figures show segment length distributions for ACS and RCS, the evolution of end-to-end distance distributions P(R) across chain lengths, and the relationship between the entropy ratio and the number of Kuhn segments per RCS.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of non-Gaussianity in short chains, defines the structural model (ACS/RCS), presents the mathematical framework of Tsallis statistics, details the MD simulation methodology, analyzes the evolution of the entropic index q and entropy ratio with chain length, and concludes with the 'statistical masking' hypothesis.
why_interesting: The study explores the emergence of extensive (Boltzmann-Gibbs) statistics from non-extensive (Tsallis) regimes via a masking mechanism, which is conceptually relevant to understanding how local correlations and non-equilibrium features are lost in large-scale many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Data histogram for the end-to-end distances of the different polymer systems and fits with a Gaussian, Eq. (1), and q-Gaussian, Eq. (8). (a) C50 and C100, (b) C250 and C500. The entropic index q values are indicated. The fit quality parameters are provided in Table III and supplementary material, Table S1.

FIG. 2. Linearity test in R2. Plots of the logarithm of the reduced density as a function of R2 and fits with the Gaussian (a) and q-Gaussian (b) as described in Fig. 1. Eq. (3) describes the Gaussian fit (solid line) and Eq. (15) describes the q-Gaussian fit. Notice the tail in all systems and superimposed fit with the q-Gaussian, except for the C500 system. Note that a plot of lnq  Pq(R)/R2 vs. R2, should yield a straight line for C50–C250, according to Eq. (15)–supplementary material, Fig. S2.

FIG. 3. Tail concentration of the last data quartile (R ≥Q75) for the different polymer systems. Blue symbols represent individual simulation snapshots (frame-by-frame, FrbyFr). The solid blue line and shaded band show the binned mean ±1 SEM. Panels (a), (b) and (c) represent the correlations between the tail concentration and q for C50, C100 and C250, respectively. Panels (d) and (e) illustrate the absence of correlation between the tail concentration and the characteristic ratio C∞for C100 and C250, respectively. Panel (f) shows the analogous result for the ACS mass fraction, illustrated for C250; the Pearson and Spearman correlation coefficients are rP = 0.013 and rS = 0.016,...

FIG. 4. Entropy non-extensivity quantified by the ratio � Sq/S1 (t) as a function of the entropic index q(t) fit for: (a) C50; (b) C100; (c) C250; and (d) C500. The time-weighted mean ⟨(Sq/S1)(t)⟩is indicated in each panel. As in Fig. 3, each data point represents an individual simulation

FIG. 5. Segment length distributions in terms of united atoms (UA) for ACS (red) and RCS (blue) in all four systems. C50 has no RCS segments. Only RCS segments containing at least two Kuhn segments (nseg ≥2nk) are included. The x-axis in UA units is directly proportional to the molar mass of the segment (M = nUA × 14.03 gmol−1 for polyethylene), so the distributions are the same as those in Fig. S6 in the supplementary material, expressed in different units. The vertical dashed and dash-dotted lines mark Mn,RCS and Mz,RCS from Table IV, converted to UA units. The distributions decay following a Flory-like form, with an increasing number of RCS segments per chain as chain length increases.
